dimensions.name: five_variable_completeness
dimensions.description: Does the learner's analysis cover all five variables — scope, schedule, cost, quality, and risk — rather than just the visible three (scope/schedule/cost)? The chapter is explicit that quality and risk are real variables, not passive outcomes.

anchors.1: Covers only scope/schedule/cost. Quality and risk absent or treated as passive outcomes.
anchors.3: Covers four of five with one variable shallow or implicit.
anchors.5: All five variables explicitly named and their current state assessed. Quality and risk get the same analytical weight as scope/schedule/cost.

dimensions.name: silent_cut_detection
dimensions.description: Does the learner identify where pressure is being absorbed silently — usually into quality or risk — when one of the other variables is under strain? This is the chapter's central skill.

anchors.1: Does not detect silent cuts; treats reported metrics at face value.
anchors.3: Notices one cut is being absorbed silently but does not trace it to a specific variable or quantify it.
anchors.5: Names the specific silent cut with quoted evidence, identifies which variable is absorbing the pressure, and quantifies the long-term cost where possible (e.g. 'compressed UAT to 60% from a planned 85%; expect 2-3 post-launch defect spikes').

dimensions.name: scope_disambiguation
dimensions.description: Does the learner distinguish product scope vs project scope vs the scope in the sponsor's head when scope is being discussed? The three are different and conflating them is a source of project trouble.

anchors.1: Uses 'scope' as a single undifferentiated concept.
anchors.3: Distinguishes two of the three senses, or distinguishes them implicitly without naming.
anchors.5: Names all three when relevant to the scenario, or two of three with the third explicitly noted as out of scope for the analysis. Catches sponsor-mental-model drift where the scenario shows it.

dimensions.name: trade_off_visibility
dimensions.description: Does the status narrative make implicit trade-offs explicit? A status report that says 'schedule amber due to integration slip, currently absorbed through compressed UAT timeline which carries a quality risk on non-core flows' is the chapter's exemplar — the trade-off is named, not hidden.

anchors.1: Status narrative is RAG summaries with no trade-off explicitness. Pressures are present but absorption is hidden.
anchors.3: Names one trade-off explicitly but treats other absorption choices as background.
anchors.5: Each pressure is followed by an explicit absorption statement: which variable is taking the pressure, why that was the choice, and what the trade-off costs. No implicit absorption.

dimensions.name: long_term_cost_tracking
dimensions.description: Does the learner connect short-term choices (compressed testing, deferred quality work, accepted risks) to the long-term cost they create (post-launch defects, follow-up projects, technical debt, security incidents)? The chapter's loyalty-platform anecdote is the model: a project that 'launched on time, in budget' but cost 30% more in true terms over 18 months.

anchors.1: No connection between current choices and downstream cost.
anchors.3: Names long-term cost in passing without quantifying or attributing it.
anchors.5: For each short-term absorption, names the likely long-term cost with rough magnitude where possible. Captures the choice in a closeout-items list or risk register so it surfaces later.
